technologies: Popunder / pop-tab ads (window.open + window.location swap, onclick-triggered, localStorage frequency cap)
In-page push / Social Bar / push notification ads
Server-Side Ad Insertion (SSAI) - legitimate AVOD path, ad-blocker resistant
Google Ad Manager / AdSense (licensed content only)
Browser crypto mining via JavaScript (Coinhive, historical)
HLS (.m3u8) + .vtt player the ads wrap around
gotchas: Google AdSense / Google Ad Manager will refuse and ban pirated anime: Google policy explicitly prohibits 'unauthorized... streaming, sharing, copying or downloading of copyrighted content' and lists anime by name; pages hit with a valid DMCA notice are permanently blocked from serving AdSense. So mainstream demand is off the table for unlicensed content - this is the core reason the niche runs on AdSense alternatives.

Anime/free-content traffic is low-quality from an advertiser view (users want free stuff, heavy ad-block usage, non-buying intent), so even accepting networks pay low CPMs - practitioners report literally cents per day on 1k-5k impressions in cheap geos.

Popunders/redirects destroy UX and are the same channel as malvertising; ~78% of pirate sites in the Digital Citizens Alliance/White Bullet study served malware-laden ads, and pop-under malvertising alone accounted for ~$88M of ~$121M/yr malvertising revenue - this is the reputational and legal liability of the model.

Heavy ad-blocker usage means client-side popunders/banners are often blocked entirely; sites respond with anti-adblock walls, in-page push, and 'MultiTag' fallbacks. Only SSAI (server-stitched) reliably defeats blockers, and that needs a real ad server + licensed content.

Payout/account risk: networks can ban or shave (withhold) earnings; pirate operators mitigate with multiple domains/mirrors and fast-payout networks (PopAds daily, HilltopAds weekly from $20) and crypto cashout, but accounts and domains get seized (HiAnime/aniwatch shut down in early 2026).
Crypto mining is no longer viable: Coinhive died in March 2019, browsers and AV flag cryptojacking, and yields were tiny relative to CPU/UX cost - effectively a dead monetization channel.

Some 'accepting' intermediaries (PropellerAds, RichAds documented in the Unholy Triangle report) knowingly approve deceptive/fake-virus creatives; building on them inherits brand-safety and possible legal exposure even before the copyright issue.

For a legitimate build: the model only flips profitable when you have licenses - then you can use VAST+SSAI+Ad Manager at $5-$15 CPM brand-safe and add an SVOD no-ads tier, but content licensing cost/availability is the real barrier, not the ad tech.

refuted.claim: Claim that HiAnime/aniwatch shut down in March 2026 came from a single search-summary source and was not independently verified by fetching the site; status changes frequently.
refuted.note: The meta-claim (that the shutdown was unverified / single-source) is now superseded. The March 13, 2026 shutdown is corroborated by MULTIPLE independent sources, including the HiAnime Wikipedia article (citing TorrentFreak's reported farewell message and AnimeMojo), with a permanent closure circa June 1, 2026 after ~80 days inactive, and USTR adding HIANIME to the Notorious Markets list in March 2026. The 'great moments / time to say goodbye' farewell text matches across sources. The underlying caution (domains change frequently; copycat hianimetv.cfd etc. are unaffiliated) remains valid, but the shutdown itself is well-established as of June 2026.

confirmed: PopAds effective CPM ~$4-$6 US/UK popunder, ~$2-$4 other geos, payout from $5 daily; figures from network/affiliate-review pages and fluctuate by season/geo/quality.
HilltopAds minimum CPMs roughly $0.10-$2.50 by geo/targeting; weekly payout, $20 minimum; from HilltopAds blog/affiliate reviews.
Real-world anime-site earnings of 'cents per day on 1k-5k impressions' come from anecdotal BHW forum posts (one cheap-geo operator), not a representative sample.

Aggregate piracy figures ($1.3B/yr pirate ad revenue; ~$121M/yr malvertising min with $88M from popunders; top-5 sites ~$18.3M each) from the 2022 DCA + White Bullet 'Unholy Triangle' report (500-site dataset, ~2020-2021), anti-piracy agenda, order-of-magnitude.

Whether the current hianime/aniwatch front end serves video pre-roll (VAST) vs only popunder/banner/push was not confirmed from live source; inferred from network format menus and scraped-m3u8 player architecture.

Specific network roster on this exact operator (which of PopAds/HilltopAds/Adsterra/PropellerAds are live today) was not confirmed by inspecting live page requests; it is the standard niche set, not a verified per-site list.
Legitimate AVOD CPM $5-$15 for mid-tier streaming is a general OTT estimate (Muvi/Perion glossaries), not anime-specific; Crunchyroll's free AVOD reportedly ending Dec 31, 2025 per CBR/Dexerto/ANN.
corrections: Claim 4 conflates two separate DCA reports. The $121M malvertising minimum AND the $88M-from-popunders figure are from the Sept 2022 'Unholy Triangle' report (500-site dataset, malware data collected April-May 2022). But the ~$1.3B/$1.34B aggregate pirate ad revenue AND the top-5-sites ~$18.3M-each figure are from the EARLIER Aug 2021 'Breaking Bads' report. Cite them as two distinct reports, not one.

The $88M popunder figure should be stated more precisely: the report says pop-under ads account for '$88 million of the $121 million they make a year from malvertising' - i.e. $88M is a subset of the malvertising total, not a separate revenue stream. The claim's phrasing ('$88M from popunders') is correct but should specify it is $88M of the $121M malvertising minimum.

Claim 5's framing is now outdated: the HiAnime March 13, 2026 shutdown is multi-source corroborated (Wikipedia/TorrentFreak/USTR Notorious Markets/multiple outlets), not a single unverified search summary. Update the manuscript to treat the shutdown as established fact with a permanent-closure date around June 1, 2026, while keeping the caveat about frequently-changing/copycat domains.

Claim 1's $4-$6 / $2-$4 CPM ranges are headline figures from affiliate-incentivized review pages; the same pages explicitly state real-world CPMs frequently fall under $1 for low-income geos. Present these as quoted maximums, not expected earnings.

PopAds is reported to have started banning warez/piracy domains, so despite being the canonical 'first network named' for piracy sites, it may not actually be live on current anime-piracy sites. This weakens any assertion that PopAds specifically monetizes these sites today (relevant to claims 1 and 7).
additionalFindings: The Unholy Triangle report names specific ad intermediaries as facilitators with dedicated case studies: PropellerAds (called among the biggest facilitators of malvertising on piracy sites) and RichAds (which investigators posing as shady advertisers found willing to serve overtly malicious creatives - 'All creatives were mainly produced by RichAds. In 2 months, there were 67 creatives total'). This directly supports the manuscript's claim that these networks accept copyright-infringing/malicious traffic, and gives named, citable primary-source evidence rather than just forum inference.

The DCA self-describes the $121M as a conservative MINIMUM, explicitly not extrapolated beyond the 500-site dataset (PDF: 'considered a minimum as it was estimated from the relatively small dataset of 500 piracy sites... This figure was not extrapolated'). This strengthens the manuscript's 'order-of-magnitude / minimum' framing and is worth quoting verbatim.

The open-source aniwatch scraper architecture is concretely documented: getEpisodeSources returns .m3u8 HLS files plus Referer/User-Agent headers and .vtt subtitles, organized by server (MegaCloud, vidstreaming) and category (sub/dub/raw); the MegaCloud extractor fetches decryption keys from an external GitHub repo. This is good primary evidence for the 'scraped-m3u8 custom player' description in the case study.

Crunchyroll is NOT fully exiting ad-supported anime - it keeps ad-supported linear FAST channels (The Crunchyroll Channel on Samsung TV Plus, LG Channels, Pluto TV, Roku Channel, Sling, Prime Video). The manuscript should say Crunchyroll is ending its free on-demand AVOD tier specifically, not abandoning AVOD entirely. Industry commentators explicitly warn this may push viewers back to piracy, which is directly relevant to the monetization-layer narrative.

Current (2026) CTV/AVOD benchmarks show premium AVOD/FAST inventory at $15-$45 CPM, well above the $5-$15 mid-tier figure. The $5-$15 range is genuinely the low/mid end and should be labeled as such; it understates what reputable premium anime AVOD inventory (e.g. Crunchyroll's own) could command.

layer: Legal landscape & the legitimate way to build a hianime-style anime streaming product
overview: The zoro.to -> aniwatch.to -> hianime.to lineage was, at its 2024-2026 peak, "almost certainly the world's largest pirate site" (364M+ monthly visits, more than Disney+), operated out of Vietnam. It is straightforward copyright infringement: it hosts/streams licensed anime without authorization and monetizes via ads. That exposure is real and escalating — USTR Notorious Markets listing (2024 and 2025), ACE/MPA/CODA takedowns, and the eventual shutdown around March-June 2026. In the US, operating a for-profit unauthorized streaming service is now a felony (Protecting Lawful Streaming Act of 2020, up to 5-10 years), and operators of comparable sites have been criminally convicted abroad (B9GOOD, Bato.to). A developer can replicate the hianime UX (fast catalog browse, AniList-rich metadata, episode grid, in-page HLS player, watch tracking) without the legal exposure by choosing a defensible content layer: a streaming-free metadata/tracker/discovery site, a self-hosted "bring your own files" media-server frontend (Jellyfin/Plex shape), aggregation of only officially-licensed free sources (Muse Asia/Ani-One YouTube, FAST channels, public domain), or — far harder — actually licensing content (library packages are the only realistic indie entry point). The most defensible indie shape is a metadata/discovery + tracker app that links out to legal sources and optionally fronts the user's own Jellyfin library.

components.name: The infringing model (what to NOT copy)
components.role: The hianime/consumet/aniwatch-api stack: scrape licensed anime, extract HLS from MegaCloud/RapidCloud, proxy m3u8/TS through your own server to defeat referer/CORS, serve to an ad-monetized SPA. This is the source of legal liability: unauthorized reproduction + public performance, and (in the US) potential criminal felony for the for-profit operator.
examples: Hosting/streaming copyrighted episodes without a license = direct infringement
Server-side m3u8 proxy that re-serves studio content = a reproduction/transmission, undercuts any 'we just link' defense
Ad monetization establishes the 'commercial' / 'financial benefit' element regulators and PLSA care about

components.name: DMCA Section 512 safe harbor (and why pirate sites do not get it)
components.role: 17 U.S.C. 512(c) shields US hosts that store user-uploaded content IF they: designate a DMCA agent with the Copyright Office, do notice-and-takedown expeditiously, have and actually enforce a repeat-infringer termination policy, lack actual/red-flag knowledge, and do not receive a financial benefit while having the right/ability to control. Sites curated by the operator (not genuinely user-uploaded) and built primarily to serve infringing content fail on knowledge, willful blindness, and the 'designed for infringement' factor.
examples: Required: registered 512 agent at copyright.gov, takedown workflow, written + enforced repeat-infringer policy
Disqualifiers: willful blindness, ad revenue tied to infringing content, curatorial control
Safe harbor protects a neutral UGC host, not an operator who hand-builds an anime catalog

components.name: Route 1 — License content (hardest)
components.role: Become a real licensee. Anime rights sit with a Japanese production committee (seisaku iinkai); deals are territory/window/platform-type specific (SVOD/AVOD/FAST). Simulcast is gatekept and expensive; the only realistic indie door is library/catalog packages of older titles.
examples: New seasonal w/ low awareness ~$15K-$100K; catalog 5+ yrs ~$5K-$50K/series
Sub-agents/distributors add 3-5 months and 15-20% markup; library packages = most accessible AVOD/FAST entry point

components.name: Route 2 — Metadata / tracker / discovery site (AniList-shape)
components.role: Build the browse/search/recommend/track UX with NO video. Pull catalog/airing/character data from AniList GraphQL (or MAL/Jikan, AniDB, TheTVDB). This is the cleanest legal posture: you reproduce facts + small cover art under license/fair-use norms, not the works themselves. Add 'where to watch' deep links to legal services.
examples: AniList GraphQL single endpoint https://graphql.anilist.co; OAuth2 for user lists
Mirror AniList's own product: lists, scores, airing schedule, recommendations
JustWatch-style 'available on Crunchyroll/Netflix/Muse Asia' availability rows

components.name: Route 3 — Self-hosted 'bring your own files' frontend (Jellyfin/Plex-shape)
components.role: Ship software, not content. A media-server frontend the user points at their OWN legally-obtained files. The user is the only one reproducing/streaming, privately, to themselves. This is the Jellyfin/Plex/Streamyfin model and it gives the full hianime in-browser-player UX legally because there is no public performance and no operator-hosted catalog.
examples: Jellyfin (GPL self-hosted server) + a custom anime-skinned web/mobile client
Anime metadata via Shokofin/Shoko, jellyfin-plugin-anime (AniList/AniDB/MAL/TVDB), MyAnimeSync for MAL progress sync
Jellyseerr-style request UI; you distribute only the app, never the episodes

components.name: Route 4 — Aggregate only legal free sources (AVOD/FAST/public domain)
components.role: Index and embed officially-uploaded free anime: licensor YouTube channels (Muse Asia/Muse Communication, Ani-One/Medialink), FAST channels (Crunchyroll Channel on Pluto TV / Roku Channel / Samsung TV Plus, RetroCrush), Tubi/Pluto, and genuine public-domain works. Use official embeds/APIs, respect geo-locks, never re-host.
examples: YouTube IFrame Player API embeds of Muse Asia / Ani-One uploads (region-locked to SEA/India/ME — geo aware)
FAST/AVOD: Crunchyroll Channel, RetroCrush, Tubi, Pluto TV anime channels
Public domain: pre-1953 / lapsed works only — narrow but zero-risk

components.name: Secondary/inducement liability (the link-out trap)
components.role: Even a streaming-free site can be liable under MGM v. Grokster inducement if its design/messaging shows intent to foster infringement plus actual infringement. Linking to known pirate streams, or marketing 'watch free full episodes', re-creates exposure. Defensibility comes from linking only to authorized sources and not designing the product around infringement.
examples: Grokster two-prong: (1) intent to cause infringement (2) actual direct infringement
'Mere knowledge of infringing potential' is NOT enough — but curated links to pirate hosts can be
Keep deep links pointed at Crunchyroll/Netflix/official YouTube, not at MegaCloud-style embeds

howItWorks: Diagnose the liability source: in the pirate stack the legal problem is the CONTENT layer (unauthorized hosting/streaming + ad monetization), not the UX layer. The browse/search/player UX is legally neutral and fully reusable.
Pick a content layer by risk tolerance: (4)+(3) for an indie = lowest risk; (2) metadata-only = very low; (1) licensing = lowest legal risk but near-impossible economics for an indie.

For the metadata/tracker product: register an AniList API client, run OAuth2, query the single GraphQL endpoint for catalog/airing/character/recommendation data, respect the 90 req/min limit and the commercial-license threshold (>$150/mo revenue requires emailing AniList for a commercial license).

Render the hianime-style UI on top of metadata: seasonal grid, search, genre filters, detail page with synopsis/characters/relations, user watchlist + scores synced via OAuth — exactly AniList's own architecture, which runs on its own public API.
Add legal 'where to watch': map each title to availability on Crunchyroll/Netflix/Muse Asia/Ani-One/FAST channels (JustWatch-style) and deep-link out; never embed pirate players.

For actual video without licensing: ship the self-hosted route — distribute a Jellyfin-backed client/skin; the user runs the server on their own hardware against their own files, enriches with Shokofin/jellyfin-plugin-anime metadata, and streams privately to themselves.

For legal free streaming inside your site: embed official sources via sanctioned mechanisms — YouTube IFrame API for Muse Asia/Ani-One, FAST/AVOD partner embeds — and enforce geo-restrictions rather than bypassing them.

If you ever host any user-contributed media: claim 512 safe harbor properly — designate a DMCA agent with the US Copyright Office, implement notice-and-takedown, publish AND enforce a repeat-infringer policy, avoid willful blindness, and do not tie ad revenue to infringing items.
Document non-infringing intent: clear ToS, authorized-source-only linking, no 'free full episodes' marketing — this is your Grokster inducement defense.

keyRepos.name: AniList API (docs + GraphQL)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Free GraphQL anime/manga metadata API (500k+ entries: titles, characters, staff, studios, airing schedule, recommendations, user lists). Single endpoint https://graphql.anilist.co, OAuth2 for user data. 90 req/min limit; commercial license required above ~$150/mo revenue. The reference architecture for a legal metadata/tracker site.

keyRepos.name: Jellyfin
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Free/GPL self-hosted media server — the legal backbone for the 'bring your own files' route. Scans the user's own library, fetches metadata, transcodes, and streams to clients; you build an anime-skinned frontend on top.

keyRepos.name: jellyfin-plugin-anime
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Anime metadata provider plugin for Jellyfin pulling from AniList/AniDB/MAL(Jikan)/TVDB/Fanart — gives a self-hosted library the rich metadata that makes a hianime-style UI possible (now archived; Shokofin is the active alternative).

keyRepos.name: awesome-jellyfin
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Curated list of Jellyfin plugins/clients/themes including anime metadata aggregators, Shokofin (Shoko Server integration), MyAnimeSync (Jellyfin<->MyAnimeList progress sync), and Streamyfin — the building blocks for a legal self-hosted anime frontend.

keyRepos.name: US Copyright Office — Section 512
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Authoritative DMCA safe-harbor resources: notice-and-takedown system, DMCA-agent designation, repeat-infringer and knowledge requirements that any UGC-hosting variant must satisfy.

keyRepos.name: Protecting Lawful Streaming Act (Wikipedia)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Summary of the 2020 US law making large-scale commercial unauthorized streaming a felony (up to 5/10 years); defines the 'commercial, primarily-for-infringement' criteria that pirate operators meet and legitimate apps must avoid.

technologies: MyAnimeList / Jikan API, AniDB, TheTVDB (alternative metadata sources)
Jellyfin (GPL self-hosted media server) + Plex/Emby ecosystem
Shokofin / Shoko Server, MyAnimeSync, Streamyfin (Jellyfin anime plugins/clients)
YouTube IFrame Player API (embedding official Muse Asia / Ani-One channels)
FAST/AVOD platforms: Pluto TV, The Roku Channel, Samsung TV Plus, Tubi, RetroCrush (Crunchyroll Channel distribution)
JustWatch-style availability mapping for legal 'where to watch' deep links
DMCA Section 512 compliance tooling (registered agent, takedown workflow, repeat-infringer policy)
HLS / video.js / hls.js player UI (the UX layer — legal regardless of source, only the content origin matters)
gotchas: Server-side m3u8/TS proxying of licensed content is itself a reproduction/transmission — it is the single most legally damning piece of the pirate stack and destroys any 'we only link' defense.

Ad monetization is what makes the operator 'commercial' under the Protecting Lawful Streaming Act and supplies the 'direct financial benefit' that breaks DMCA safe harbor — monetizing infringing content is uniquely dangerous.

DMCA safe harbor does NOT cover an operator-curated catalog; it only covers genuinely user-uploaded content with takedown + enforced repeat-infringer policy + no willful blindness. Pirate sites fail every prong.

Linking out is not automatically safe: under MGM v. Grokster, intent-to-induce + actual infringement creates secondary liability, so a 'tracker' that deep-links to pirate embeds or markets 'free full episodes' re-acquires the exposure it tried to avoid.
AniList is free only for non-commercial use; above ~$150/mo revenue you must obtain a commercial license (email AniList) — a metadata business cannot just scrape and monetize.

Official free YouTube channels (Muse Asia, Ani-One) are heavily geo-locked to SEA/India/Middle East; a US/EU-facing aggregator will hit region blocks, and bypassing geo-restrictions reintroduces ToS/legal problems.

Crunchyroll removed its free ad-supported tier on Jan 1, 2026 (free anime now mainly via FAST channels), so 'free legal Crunchyroll embed' is no longer a viable building block — verify current tier status.
Crunchyroll has no official public developer API/partner program; community libraries are reverse-engineered and violate ToS — not a legitimate integration path.
Public domain anime is a very narrow set (lapsed/very old works) — not a catalog you can build a hianime-scale product on.

Indie licensing is realistically limited to cheap catalog/library packages; production committees gate simulcast, and sub-agents add months + 15-20% markup, so the economics rarely close for a solo dev.

Enforcement is real and rising: USTR Notorious Markets listings, ACE/MPA/CODA coordinated takedowns (AnimeFlix, Fmovies/Aniwave, 16 Brazil sites), and criminal convictions of operators abroad (B9GOOD operator jailed; Bato.to operator arrested).

confirmed: Shutdown dates: hianime went offline ~March 13, 2026 and permanently shut ~June 1, 2026; status fluid/disputed.
USTR Notorious Markets listed hianime in BOTH the 2024 and 2025 reports.
'World's largest pirate site' / 364M+ monthly visits surpassing Disney+; figures are SimilarWeb-style estimates that vary by month/source.
Crunchyroll removed its free ad-supported tier effective Jan 1, 2026 (free streaming ended Dec 31, 2025).
Anime licensing price bands ($15K-$100K new seasonal, $5K-$50K catalog, $1M-$5M+ tier-1 simulcast) come from one vendor (Vitrina) blog and are illustrative ranges, not authoritative rate cards.
AniList rate limit = 90 req/min and commercial threshold ~$150/mo revenue.
jellyfin-plugin-anime is archived; Shokofin and the awesome-jellyfin anime aggregator are the current maintained paths.
corrections: USTR wording: The phrase 'priority notorious streaming site' / 'Priority' does NOT appear in the body of the 2025 USTR Notorious Markets PDF (0 hits when extracting the full 61-page text). That designation comes from press coverage (CBR/FandomWire/GameRant), not the USTR document itself. Do not attribute 'priority' to the USTR report as a quote.

AniList rate limit nuance: 90 req/min is the NOMINAL limit, but AniList's own docs state the API is 'currently in a degraded state and limited to 30 requests per minute.' Any build-time implementation should target 30/min, not 90/min, until AniList lifts the degraded state.

Jellyfin deprecation pointer: jellyfin-plugin-anime's official replacement list is the four split plugins (jellyfin-plugin-anidb, -anisearch, -anilist, -kitsu), NOT Shokofin. Shokofin is a separate maintained path (Shoko-Server backed). Both are current, but they are distinct recommendations — don't conflate the archive note with Shokofin.

Traffic figure precision: the '364M' peak is specifically October 2024 (TorrentFreak); it is not a stable monthly average. The site's '209.5M average (Jun-Aug 2024)' and ~244M (Aug 2025) are different snapshots. The researcher's '364M+ monthly' should be qualified as a peak month, not a sustained figure.

Crunchyroll free-tier date precision: free ad-supported streaming ended December 31, 2025; paid subscription became mandatory January 1, 2026. Both framings ('ends Dec 31, 2025' and 'removed Jan 1, 2026') are correct and refer to the same change.
additionalFindings: The 2024 USTR PDF confirms the FULL lineage in the researcher's context as an official-source quote: zoro.to (shut down July 2023 by rightsholders/anti-piracy associations) -> aniwatch.to -> HiAnime (rebranded March 2024), 'run from Vietnam.' This corroborates the 'operated out of Vietnam' and the zoro->aniwatch->hianime chain directly from a primary government source, not just press.

Aniwatch (predecessor) was on the 2023 USTR NML, so the brand family has been USTR-listed across 2023 (Aniwatch), 2024 (HiAnime), and 2025 (HiAnime) — three consecutive reports under the lineage, stronger than the '2024 and 2025' claim implies.

ACE/anti-piracy escalation corroborated: secondary sources tie the March 13, 2026 shutdown timing to an ACE-secured $18.75M US District Court judgment against a piracy operator reported around the same date — consistent with the researcher's 'ACE/MPA/CODA takedowns' narrative (though the $18.75M judgment's exact defendant linkage to HiAnime is press-asserted, not verified to a court docket here).
USTR 2025 list scope: the 2025 Notorious Markets List identifies 37 online + 32 physical markets (per CBR summary of the report), useful context for how selective the listing is.

Defensibility note for the legit-path thesis: the Crunchyroll free-tier removal (Jan 1 2026) is repeatedly cited in coverage as a piracy-driver risk — which strengthens the market case for the researcher's proposed legal metadata/discovery + 'bring your own files' (Jellyfin/Plex-shape) + officially-licensed-free-source aggregation app, since the legal free entry point in the market just narrowed.

AniList Terms of Use page (docs.anilist.co/guide/terms-of-use) returns HTTP 403 to automated fetch; the $150/month commercial-license term was verified via the GitBook mirror and consistent search snippets rather than a direct fetch of the canonical page. A human should confirm on the live page before relying on the exact figure contractually.
